coredata.dc:title: Trustworthy battery state of charge estimation enabled by multi-task deep learning

coredata.dc:description: The accurate estimation of state of charge (SOC) is critical for the reliable operation and safety of lithium-ion batteries. However, unexpected operating conditions, such as dynamic temperature variations and sensor faults, pose significant challenges to the reliability of data-driven estimation methods. This work addresses these challenges by proposing a novel multi-task deep neural network (DNN) framework that integrates a plug-and-play anomaly detection module to enable trustworthy SOC estimation. The proposed model employs an encoder to capture temporal dependencies from input voltage and current signals, followed by two parallel modules for SOC estimation and anomaly detection. The anomaly detection module reconstructs the input voltage using the learnt features and current sequence, identifying unreliable SOC estimations based on large reconstruction errors. Experimental validation using 188,157 discharging data points demonstrates the model's robustness under diverse unexpected conditions. The SOC estimation root mean squared error (RMSE) is reduced from 4.38 % to 0.86 % under temperature variations, and significant improvements are also observed in the presence of sensor faults. Additionally, its generalisation capability is demonstrated through validation on a public dataset featuring a different battery type. These results highlight the proposed method's effectiveness in enhancing both the accuracy and reliability of SOC estimation. The predictive performance is further rationalised by visualising the extracted latent features, providing deeper insights into the model's capabilities. The proposed multi-task learning paradigm has the potential to be adapted for trustworthy estimation of other battery states.

The kernel uses the radial basis function (RBF) and the kernel coefficient is calculated by 1/(2 × variance of input data).

head.ce:title: Trustworthy battery state of charge estimation enabled by multi-task deep learning

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The accurate estimation of state of charge (SOC) is critical for the reliable operation and safety of lithium-ion batteries. However, unexpected operating conditions, such as dynamic temperature variations and sensor faults, pose significant challenges to the reliability of data-driven estimation methods. This work addresses these challenges by proposing a novel multi-task deep neural network (DNN) framework that integrates a plug-and-play anomaly detection module to enable trustworthy SOC estimation. The proposed model employs an encoder to capture temporal dependencies from input voltage and current signals, followed by two parallel modules for SOC estimation and anomaly detection. The anomaly detection module reconstructs the input voltage using the learnt features and current sequence, identifying unreliable SOC estimations based on large reconstruction errors. Experimental validation using 188,157 discharging data points demonstrates the model's robustness under diverse unexpected conditions. The SOC estimation root mean squared error (RMSE) is reduced from 4.38 % to 0.86 % under temperature variations, and significant improvements are also observed in the presence of sensor faults. Additionally, its generalisation capability is demonstrated through validation on a public dataset featuring a different battery type. These results highlight the proposed method's effectiveness in enhancing both the accuracy and reliability of SOC estimation. The predictive performance is further rationalised by visualising the extracted latent features, providing deeper insights into the model's capabilities. The proposed multi-task learning paradigm has the potential to be adapted for trustworthy estimation of other battery states.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries represent one of the most promising energy storage solutions for electric vehicles (EVs), characterised by their merits including high energy density, long lifespan, and low cost [1–3]. However, they also pose potential risks such as thermal runaway, capacity degradation, and safety hazards, which, if unaddressed, may lead to catastrophic failures [4]. Ensuring the safe and reliable operation of lithium-ion batteries requires continuous monitoring of key battery states [5]. Among these, the state of charge (SOC), defined as the ratio of the available capacity to the maximum capacity, is particularly important but cannot be directly measured by sensors. Instead, it must be estimated using measurable signals including voltage and current [6,7]. Therefore, it is essential to develop an accurate and reliable SOC estimation model.

Ampere-hour counting (AHC) is a basic estimation method that computes the SOC variation by integrating the measured current over time to track the SOC variation [8]. However, its performance is negatively impacted by errors in the initial SOC and sampling noise. The open circuit voltage (OCV) method is used to calibrate the initial SOC of the AHC method by looking up the OCV-SOC table [9,10]. Nevertheless, the OCV can only be used after the batteries rest for a long period. To address the challenge, Kalman filters and other filters have been used to combine AHC and battery models [11–13]. SOC can be online estimated by correcting the AHC results with voltage simulation error. Chen et al. [14] developed a second-order-resistance-capacitor model to simulate battery behaviour during the charging and discharging processes. Accurate SOC estimation across a wide temperature range was subsequently achieved using an extended particle filter. However, their practical applications are hindered by cumbersome characterisation tests and inaccurate battery models [15,16], rendering them often incapable of providing reliable SOC estimation in many cases.

Data-driven methods, including machine learning and deep learning methods, have recently been extensively used in SOC estimation with superior performance [17–19]. The machine learning models, such as support vector regression (SVR) and relevance vector machine (RVM), have simpler structures without numerous stacked layers compared to the deep learning models [20,21]. They generally map the correlative features of the measured signals instead of the signals themselves to the SOC because of their poor ability of feature extraction. For example, Bianchi et al. [22] trained the SVR using application-independent constant current data and the SOC estimation further involved a post-processing to enhance the estimation accuracy. Chen et al. [23] developed a multi-kernel RVM (MKRVM) for SOC estimation, incorporating a whale optimisation algorithm to optimise the weights and kernel parameters of the MKRVM, resulting in improved estimation accuracy. Though the machine learning methods achieve satisfactory SOC estimation results, they demand cumbersome feature engineering. Instead, the deep learning methods can effectively map measurable signals to SOC by using deep neural networks (DNNs), due to their automatic feature extraction and powerful regression capability [24]. As a result, SOC estimators can be quickly developed for various types of batteries with high accuracy. For example, Fu et al. [25] proposed a radial basis function neural network for SOC estimation, with its hyperparameters optimised using the golden section method and the sparrow search algorithm. This approach improves estimation performance by efficiently identifying optimal hyperparameters. Wang et al. [26,27] and Chen et al. [28] demonstrated that the long short-term memory (LSTM) and gated recurrent unit (GRU) recurrent neural networks (RNNs) are effective and generalisable in SOC estimation under various operational conditions. LSTM and GRU networks employ "gate" mechanisms to filter irrelevant information from the current input and historical states while preserving essential information, thereby effectively addressing issues such as gradient vanishing and explosion [26,29]. Their bidirectional architectures, bidirectional LSTM (BLSTM) and bidirectional GRU (BGRU), are also reported performing accurately on SOC estimation [30,31]. Hannan et al. [32] and How et al. [33] proposed a convolutional neural network (CNN) and a fully connected neural network (FCNN) to estimate SOC and achieved root mean squared errors (RMSEs) within 4 % under diverse temperature and dynamic drive cycle conditions. In addition to these commonly used neural networks, hybrid SOC estimation models have been developed to harness the strengths of multiple components. For example, Li et al. [34] combined a temporal convolutional network (TCN) with an attention mechanism and the particle swarm optimisation (PSO) algorithm for SOC estimation. In their approach, the TCN captures the temporal dependencies between input variables and SOC, the attention mechanism enables the model to focus on critical input features, and the PSO algorithm optimises the hyperparameters of the TCN. They further demonstrated the potential of integrating the 2RC model with the TCN and PSO for enhanced SOC estimation [35]. These studies collectively highlight the promise of deep learning methods as powerful and versatile tools for SOC estimation.

However, existing data-driven methods for battery SOC estimation are evaluated under the ideal condition that the training and test datasets share a similar distribution. Although this assumption holds in most cases, the battery systems may experience unexpected operational conditions. For example, field data inevitably suffers from poor sampling accuracy, noise pollution, missing values, etc., due to sensor faults [36,37]. Hwang et al. [38] used deep learning methods to estimate the SOC at normal operating conditions and simulated sensor failures. The results show that the mean absolute error of an LSTM method at normal condition is 0.3 % while that of the LSTM method at a voltage sensor failure simulated by Gaussian distribution noise is as large as 30.39 %. It demonstrates that the DNN will suffer from unreliable SOC estimation in the presence of unexpected sensor faults. Though some approaches are applied to mitigating the effect of noise uncertainty on SOC estimation [39–41], they are tailored to certain noise situations that cannot satisfy the complex environment of sensor usage. On the other hand, battery performance is sensitive to variations in ambient temperatures since the internal electrochemical reactions are subjected to temperatures [42,43]. In practice, the battery might experience unexpected temperature variation due to incapable thermal management or abnormal heat generation [44,45]. Although such temperature variations might not directly risk battery safety, they may lead to unreliable state estimation and subsequent incorrect management strategies. Unfortunately, it is difficult to monitor the temperature of all batteries in a battery pack since rare temperature sensors are deployed, where only ∼1/10 of batteries on average are directly measured by temperature sensors [46]. Most data-driven SOC estimation methods use experimental data at a fixed temperature range as the training dataset, and the test dataset is generated at the same temperature range [47]. Although this can ensure the model generalisation in the given conditions, the trained model is challenged when the battery temperature is significantly distinct to the training dataset [48,49]. The domain adaption method is used to alleviate this issue [50,51]. However, the predefined target domain for model training still cannot satisfy all possible temperature conditions in practice.

The above analysis highlights the necessity of trustworthy SOC estimation, ensuring reliable and accurate performance even in the presence of unexpected temperatures and sensor faults. As previous machine learning methods estimate the SOC without concerning the reliability of the estimation results, it is urgent to design a model to not only estimate the SOC but also flag unreliable estimations. Given this, this study proposes a multi-task deep learning framework for trustworthy SOC estimation. The developed model simultaneously estimates SOC and reconstructs input voltage sequences, using voltage reconstruction errors as indicators to flag unreliable estimations by comparing them with a predefined threshold. This mechanism ensures reliable SOC estimation by promptly detecting and excluding unreliable results. The contributions of the proposed method are summarised as follows.
(1)
A multi-task DNN is developed to enhance SOC estimation while simultaneously ensuring robust anomaly detection. The demand for cumbersome battery tests under abnormal conditions for model training is eliminated thanks to a self-supervised learning structure.
(2)
The developed model can effectively detect abnormal input under unexpected temperature and sensor fault conditions. By filtering the estimation results of the detected abnormal input, the accuracy of the proposed method improves by over 59 % for various unexpected conditions compared with the traditional SOC estimation scheme without anomaly detection.
(3)
The method is highly flexible in both structure and application. The anomaly detection module is flexible enough to be deployed on different DNNs. Besides, the proposed multitask learning paradigm can be applied to different battery state monitoring tasks besides SOC estimation.

Battery tests were conducted to simulate EV operations under various temperature and drive cycle conditions, generating two datasets containing 188,157 pieces of discharging data to validate the proposed method. The generalisation and robustness of the proposed method are evaluated under various unexpected temperatures and sensor faults. In addition, the superiority of the method is rationalised by visualising the distributions of latent features.

Current SOC estimation models built with DNNs generally follow an end-to-end structure, which does not provide an indication of the reliability of the estimation. To address this limitation, the existing models are improved by designing a two-branch model that simultaneously estimates SOC and detects abnormal input. As shown in Fig. 1 , the proposed method starts with online data collection. Current and voltage are commonly used as input variables for SOC estimation, as the current is directly related to the SOC over time, while the voltage responses provide valuable insights into the battery's operational status. Temperature is not included as an input because surface temperature is affected by both the thermal management system and the accumulation of generated heat [52]. Same as existing DNN-based SOC estimation methods, the voltage and current sampled in sequences in a short window serve as input data [53]. A sliding window is employed to create input segments from the experimental data sequence [54]. The battery surface temperature is influenced not only by heat generation but also by thermal management and ambient temperature. Furthermore, the voltage response, which varies with temperature, provides valuable information about the battery's thermal state, making the surface temperature unnecessary as an input for the proposed model. The output of the encoder network is then directed to two branches of SOC estimation and anomaly detection. The anomaly detection is achieved by reconstructing the input voltage sequence, as SOC is closely associated with the OCV [55], while current is subjected to battery usage. The reconstruction error is used to flag the reliability of the estimated SOC. A threshold is established as a criterion, where the estimated SOC is retained if the overall error of the reconstructed voltages is below the threshold; otherwise, it is discarded. In this way, trustworthy SOC estimations are achieved. The above procedure can be mathematically expressed as

The model simultaneously outputs both SOC estimation and voltage reconstruction error when fed with current and voltage samples measured in practical applications. The reconstructed voltage error effectively flags unreliable SOC estimations, ensuring the overall reliability of the SOC prediction.

The designed structure leads to the following three advantages. First, the method integrates SOC estimation and anomaly detection tasks in one network without the need to develop two networks independently, improving the efficiency of model development. Second, compared to traditional supervised methods [56], the model is only trained on data of general battery usage thanks to the self-supervised model structure, eliminating cumbersome battery tests under abnormal operating conditions. Moreover, unlike other unsupervised diagnostic methods, such as isolation forests and k-nearest neighbours [57,58], which fail to identify the reliability of an input in real time, the proposed model instantaneously evaluates the trustworthiness of the input. Third, since the model is trained exclusively on normal data, it is not restricted to specific abnormal battery usage patterns and can effectively detect unknown faults by generating large voltage reconstruction errors for anomalous operational data.

The proposed model consists of four main modules: an encoder module, a decoder module, an SOC estimation module, and an anomaly detection module. Each of these modules plays a distinct role in the overall system and is described in the following subsections.

The encoder module is used to extract meaningful features from the input voltage and current. The LSTM layer is used to build an encoder module as it can extract time-dependent features as hidden states at each time step. The hidden state of the encoder module can be expressed as

The SOC estimation module is used to map the relationship between the last hidden state of the encoder module and the SOC. Since the input sequence has been processed by the encoder module, three dense layers, including two hidden layers and an output layer, are used here to simply map the hidden state to the SOC at the last time step of the input sequence, which is expressed as

The decoder module is used to detect abnormal input. Since the model is designed to train only on normal data, the anomaly detection is realised by reconstructing the input voltage sequence. After the model is trained on normal data, an abnormal input will result in a large reconstruction error, which can be used to achieve anomaly detection. In contrast, the current, which is subjected to the battery load, has a direct impact on voltage fluctuations. Therefore, another input, the current sequence, is used to provide information of battery power to accurately reconstruct the voltage sequence. The voltage reconstruction result is used to assess whether the estimated SOC is reliable. The root mean squared error (RMSE) is utilised to evaluate the reconstruction fidelity, which is calculated as

The trained model produces a lower reconstruction RMSE for normal input and a higher one for abnormal input. Given that abnormal situations occur infrequently, untrustworthy SOC estimations are discarded, retaining only the reliable ones to enhance the overall reliability of the SOC estimation. To achieve this, an RMSE threshold
R M S
E
u p
should be determined to quantitatively determine the boundary between reliable and unreliable SOC estimation. In this way, the unreliable SOC estimation can be filtered when
R M S
E k
> R M S
E
u p
. If the SOC estimation is found to be unreliable, it is excluded from the decision-making process, and the system will rely on the next reliable SOC estimation. This ensures that only trustworthy SOC predictions are used, improving the overall system performance. The threshold should be larger than most of the reconstruction errors of the normal input while smaller than that of the abnormal input to ensure both reliability and detecting accuracy. An inter-quartile range (IQR) method [60] here is applied to determine the threshold based on the training dataset. First, the reconstruction RMSEs of all inputs from the training dataset are calculated. Then the first quartile
Q 1
and the third quartile
Q 3
are obtained from the RMSE distribution, and the IQR is defined as
I Q R = Q

. An appropriate threshold is determined by

u p
=
Q 3
+ 5 I Q R
in this work, which can ensure both reliability and detecting accuracy. In real-world application, this threshold is determined using a small portion of a dataset containing abnormal signals, ensuring that the majority of normal inputs are retained while effectively excluding nearly all abnormal inputs.

The proposed model can simultaneously perform the tasks of SOC estimation and anomaly detection, enabling real-time SOC estimation in practical applications. While SOC estimation serves as the primary task, voltage reconstruction is incorporated for anomaly detection. The model is trained to optimise the model parameters by narrowing the loss between the model's outputs and real values. There are two losses used to optimise the parameters of the two model branches. The first loss is defined by the mean squared error (MSE) of the reconstructed voltage and the real voltage values while the second loss is the MSE of the SOC estimation and the real SOC. denotes the i-th sample.

The overall training loss is a weighted summation of the two losses:
(8)
J =
(
1 − λ
)
J
S O C
+ λ
J V
where
λ
is a loss weight in the range of [0,1] to balance the two tasks.
λ ∈
(
0 , 1
)
is a general situation of simultaneously implementing the tasks of SOC estimation and anomaly detection. The weight
λ
is manually selected in this work and will be further explored for adaptive adjustment using advanced methods, such as homoscedastic uncertainty [61].
λ = 0
and
λ = 1
are two extreme cases in which the model only performs the SOC estimation or anomaly detection, respectively.

The model hyperparameters are introduced as follows. The input window size is set to 50 s, and the loss weight
λ
is set to 0.5 unless otherwise stated. The number of neurons of the encoder and decoder LSTM is set to 64, and the SOC estimation module has 3 dense layers, which have 10, 10, and 1 neurons, respectively. Three rectified linear unit (ReLU) activations are applied to the three dense layers to enhance the nonlinearity. The neuron number of the dense layer of the anomaly detection task is set to 1. The Adam optimiser is selected to optimise the model parameters during model training and the learning rate is set to 10−3. The training epoch is set to 100 and the input batch size is set to 128, respectively. Dominant data-driven models without anomaly detection are used for comparison, and their hyperparameters are listed in Table 1 . The other settings of the traditional deep learning models not mentioned are kept consistent with the proposed method

All the DNN models are developed and evaluated with TensorFlow (version 2.6) in Python (version 3.8). The training hardware is a laptop configured with an AMD Ryzen 76800H CPU and an NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3060 GPU.

A battery discharging dataset is collected from experiments simulating EV operations under complex conditions involving various drive cycles and temperatures. The dataset contains 120,825 data points at three temperatures, covering five different dynamic drive cycles created from four standard drive cycles.

Battery tests were performed at Shandong Provincial New Energy Power Systems Specialty Laboratory at Weifang University. The experimental devices are displayed in Fig. 2 (a), including a thermal chamber, a charging and discharging device, and a desktop computer. The thermal chamber is used to control the temperature of the batteries placed in it. The charging and discharging device provides power to batteries, which is controlled by the computer. The discharging voltage and current of the batteries are measured by sensors and are recorded by the computer at a 1 Hz frequency. The current and voltage errors caused by the device are 0.02 % of the full scale, corresponding to 0.012 A and 0.001 V, respectively.

Fifteen lithium-ion batteries are used to validate the developed model and the specifications of the batteries are shown in Table 2 . These batteries are divided into three sets, each of which is tested at 0 °C, 10 °C, and 25 °C. The battery charging and discharging are implemented as Fig. 2(e). The battery temperature of the charging process is set to 25 °C to ensure all batteries have the same charging condition. Then, the batteries rest for an hour to make the temperature uniformly distributed in the batteries. After that, the batteries are fully charged with a constant current constant voltage (CCCV) scheme. Specifically, the batteries are charged by 1C constant current until the voltage reaches the upper voltage limit, i.e., 4.2 V, followed by a 4.2 V charging until the current reaches 0.05 A. After the charging process, the temperature is set to 0 °C, 10 °C, or 25 °C and the batteries rest for another 1 h to ensure the temperature is uniformly distributed and keep the relaxing voltage stable. Then, the batteries are fully discharged until the voltage decreases to the lower voltage limit, i.e., 2.5 V. Dynamic discharging current rates are used to simulate battery usage in electric vehicles, which always work at complex temperatures and unknown sensor faults. The dynamic discharging current is generated by randomly combining four standard drive cycles: the Highway Fuel Economy Test (HWFET), Urban Dynamometer Driving Schedule (UDDS), Los Angeles 92 (LA92), and United States 06 (US06). These standard drive cycles are commonly used to simulate various driving conditions of EVs, including highway and urban road scenarios. The voltage and current of the batteries discharged at 0 °C, 10 °C, and 25 °C are shown in Fig. 2 (b), (c), and (d) respectively. These figures illustrate that the batteries are subjected to randomly varying dynamic current during discharge. Notably, positive current values are observed during energy recovery from braking. The training dataset consists of data collected from four batteries operating at 25 °C, while data from other batteries is utilised to generate the test datasets. To prevent overfitting, 10 % of the training dataset is allocated for validation, enabling the evaluation of SOC estimation results at each training epoch.

Existing DNNs suffer from unreliable SOC estimations under unexpected temperature and sensor fault conditions. The proposed method is validated in two aspects. The data collected at 25 °C is the normal data which is used to train the developed model. The trained model is first evaluated on two abnormal datasets generated from mixing a normal test data into 0 °C and 10 °C test data, respectively. Another validation is implemented on four datasets created by mixing a normal test data into four types of sensor fault data introduced in Section 4.2. The influence of the input length and the weight between SOC estimation and anomaly detection are further investigated. Additionally, the feature extraction of the anomaly detection module is visualised to explain the superiority of the proposed method.

In each case study, the SOC estimation results are compared with the actual values to evaluate the model's performance. Three metrics of RMSE and mean absolute error (MAE) are used to quantitatively assess the estimation accuracy. Specifically, RMSE measures the fluctuations and MAE evaluates the average error. The formulas for calculating these metrics are provided as follows.
(9)

)

where
N
represents the total number of input samples,
Z ˆ
denotes the estimated SOC values, and
Z
refers to the actual SOC values.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Trustworthy SOC estimation at various temperatures
ce:section.ce:para: As the temperature affects the battery characteristics and may result in unreliable SOC estimation, the SOC estimation performance of the developed method is first validated under abnormal ambient temperatures that differ from those in the training dataset. Shen et al. [62] have proved that the SOC estimation error is significantly larger when the test dataset differs from the training dataset in temperature. Their results suggest that when the DNN is trained on the dataset collected at 25 °C, the RMSE of the test data at 25 °C is 1.07 % while that of 10 °C, 0 °C, −10 °C, and −20 °C is 21.6 %. To evaluate the proposed method under unexpected temperature conditions, the developed model is trained at 25 °C and tested on two datasets at unexpected temperatures. The first test dataset is a mixture of samples at 25 °C and 10 °C and the second one is a mixture of samples at 25 °C and 0 °C, namely the 10 °C test dataset and 0 °C test dataset, respectively. Since unexpected temperature fluctuations can occur at any SOC in real-world applications, the 0 °C and 10 °C test datasets are incorporated into the 25 °C test dataset, covering their full SOC range of 0–100 %.

The reliability of the SOC estimation is indicated by the anomaly detection. Given a threshold defined by the voltage reconstruction RMSE of the training dataset, the test data with a voltage reconstruction RMSE lower than the threshold is flagged as a normal input to achieve reliable SOC estimation. Otherwise, the input data leading to a voltage reconstruction RMSE larger than the threshold is recognised as abnormal input data resulting in unreliable SOC estimation. The voltage reconstruction RMSE of the normal and abnormal inputs identified by the threshold is shown in Fig. 3 (a). The identified normal and abnormal inputs corresponding to reliable and unreliable SOC estimations are displayed in Fig. 3(b). The results show that the identified reliable SOC estimation closely tracks the real value, while the unreliable SOC estimation deviates from the real value. The unreliable SOC estimation detected by the anomaly detection is eliminated to achieve more reliable estimations. The proposed method is further compared with traditional data-driven methods without anomaly detection, including LSTM, GRU, CNN, FCNN, and SVR. The RMSE of the SOC estimations is summarised in Table 3 . Taking the LSTM as an example, the RMSEs of the 0 °C and 10 °C test datasets without anomaly detection are 4.38 % and 2.16 %, respectively. After incorporating anomaly detection, these values are significantly reduced to 0.86 %, representing improvements of 80 % and 60 %, respectively. Similarly, the MAEs for the 0 °C and 10 °C test datasets without anomaly detection are 2.73 % and 1.45 %, respectively, whereas both decrease to 0.61 % with anomaly detection. The absolute SOC estimation errors of the proposed method with anomaly detecting and the LSTM without anomaly detecting are shown in Fig. 3(c). The absolute errors are within 3 % after anomaly detection while those without anomaly detection reach 20 % and 10 %, respectively. Traditional DNN without anomaly detection assumes all estimations are trustworthy, which leads to high estimation errors in the presence of abnormal input. In comparison, the proposed method can effectively identify normal and abnormal input data, realising trustworthy SOC estimation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Trustworthy SOC estimation in case of sensor faults
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to the variations in temperature, battery state estimation is also subject to possible sensor faults. This section validates the SOC estimation in the presence of various sensor faults. Existing fault data generation methods using abuse tests have two limitations. First, the faults are allowed to occur at specific range of SOC, which cannot be used to evaluate the model performance at any SOC. Second, most studies take only one or a few fault types into consideration, in which case the method cannot be comprehensively assessed. In view of this, test datasets covering various types of faults at all SOCs are generated from the normal test data at 25 °C.

As suggested by Zhao et al. [63], sensor faults will result in noise, bias, drift, and freezing in the data measurement. Aligning with their application, four fault test datasets are generated from the 25 °C test dataset. These fault types represent most practical faults and are sufficient for evaluating the performance of our proposed method. To examine the effectiveness of the proposed method in detecting the fault data, only the second half of each test segment is replaced by the four types of fault data while the first half remains unchanged. The generation of the four types of fault data is described as follows. The noise voltage data is generated by mixing Gaussian white noise with a 50 signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) into the measured voltage, where a 1 V voltage is mixed into only 0.02 V voltage in average. The bias voltage data is generated from the measured voltage by decreasing 0.1 V on the whole. The two cases are possible to happen during data collection or signal disturbances. The drift voltage data is generated from the measured voltage by linearly decreasing the voltage from 0 to 0.1 V. Compared with the voltage drops caused by hard short circuits, the gradual voltage reduction is more difficult to detect, which can validate the effectiveness of the proposed method in the case of slight data deviation. The freezing voltage data is kept consistent with the last voltage data of the first half of voltage data, simulating the continuous sensor failure. An input sample of the four types of fault test datasets and the original data are shown in Fig. 4 (a).

To evaluate the effective anomaly detection ability of the proposed method, the four fault datasets are mixed with the normal test dataset at 25 °C respectively to form four new test datasets. The SOC estimation results of the four test datasets are shown in Fig. 4(b). Without re-training the model on data collected in sensor fault situations, the proposed method can effectively identify the unreliable SOC estimation, realising trustworthy SOC estimation. The model can detect unreliable SOC estimations even in the case of only 0.1 V voltage bias or drift. Taking the LSTM as an example, the RMSE of the SOC estimation of the proposed method with anomaly detection for the four test datasets is 0.86 %, while that without detecting the abnormal input data is 2.62 %, 8.45 %, 6.64 %, and 6.91 %, respectively. The RMSE of the proposed method decreases by 67 %, 90 %, 87 %, and 88 % for the four datasets compared with the traditional SOC estimation scheme without considering anomaly detection. Similarly, the MAE of the proposed method across the four datasets is 0.61 %, reflecting reductions of 64 %, 90 %, 87 %, and 84 %, respectively, compared to those of the method without anomaly detection. The absolute SOC estimation errors of the proposed method and the LSTM without anomaly detection are shown in Fig. 4(c). The absolute errors of the proposed method are within 2.6 % for the four datasets, while the maximum absolute error is over 15 % for the four datasets.

The proposed method is further compared with traditional approaches, including GRU, CNN, FCNN, and SVR, on datasets containing sensor faults. The comparison results, summarised in Table 3, demonstrate that the proposed method consistently outperforms these traditional machine learning models that lack anomaly detection capabilities. These findings highlight the effectiveness of the proposed framework in improving SOC estimation accuracy by incorporating anomaly detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of the training loss weight
ce:section.ce:para:
The proposed trustworthy SOC estimation is realised by simultaneously detecting the abnormal input via supervised learning and SOC estimation. The weight parameter
λ
in equation (8) is used to balance the tasks of SOC estimation and anomaly detection. Therefore, different
λ
values probably lead to different anomaly detection and SOC estimation accuracy. In particular,
λ = 0
and
λ = 1
are two extreme cases.
λ = 0
represents the anomaly detection task is suppressed and all estimations are served as reliable results.
λ = 1
means the SOC estimation task is suppressed and the model only focuses on the anomaly detection task.
λ ∈
(
0 , 1
)
leads to a general solution in which the model can simultaneously detect abnormal input and estimate SOC. To evaluate the influence of the
λ
on SOC estimation, a series of
λ
values in the range [0, 1] with a step size of 0.1 are used to train and validate the developed model separately.

The model is first trained using the training dataset at 25 °C by setting the
λ
to 0, 0.1, 0.2, …, and 1, respectively, obtaining eleven independently trained models. Taking the 0 °C test dataset as an example, the absolute SOC estimation errors of the eleven cases are displayed in Fig. 5 and their RMSEs are summarised in Table 4 . In the general situations of
λ ∈
[
0.1 , 0.9
]
, the absolute SOC estimation errors of the proposed method with anomaly detection are lower than 5 %. In comparison, the maximum absolute errors of the model without anomaly detection for these cases reach 20 % overall, demonstrating the superiority of the proposed multitask learning scheme. The proposed method with anomaly detection achieves an RMSE below 1.8 % across all general cases, compared to over 3.9 % for the model without anomaly detection. Similarly, the MAE of the proposed method remains within 1.7 %, while that of the model without anomaly detection exceeds 2.7 %. Overall, the proposed approach reduces RMSE and MAE by more than 59 % and 47 %, respectively, compared to the traditional scheme without anomaly detection in general cases.

On the other hand, the two extreme situations show poor estimation performance. When
λ = 0
, the SOC estimation with anomaly detection is equal to that without anomaly detection since the anomaly detection task is suppressed. The RMSE and MAE are 4.16 % and 2.89 %, respectively, which are larger than those of the nine general cases with anomaly detection. When
λ = 1
, the absolute SOC estimation error is over 25 % and the RMSE and MAE of with and without anomaly detections are 57 % and 49 %, respectively. It is almost impossible to accurately estimate the SOC in this case, necessitating the SOC estimation task in the developed model.

The overall results also show that the RMSE of the proposed method is in the range of [0.76 %, 1.74 %] for the nine general cases, suggesting that the proposed method is robust when the weight
λ
varying in the range of
[
0.1 , 0.9
]
. In addition, the absence of any one task of the developed model will result in unacceptable poor estimation accuracy.

The input of the developed model is not limited to a certain window size in this work. Primary validations with an input window of 50 s indicate satisfying performance of the proposed method. Since the window size should be decided by balancing the available input data and computational costs, it is essential to discuss its influence on SOC estimation. To this end, a series of window sizes of [10, 20, …, 100] s is selected to investigate the influence of the input data length on the SOC estimation accuracy. The LSTM without anomaly detection is used as an example to compare its performance with the proposed method, with varying input data lengths, in terms of accuracy and computing time.

The developed models are trained on the training dataset at 25 °C using the ten window sizes separately. The models with and without anomaly detection are compared to evaluate the proposed method. Taking the 0 °C test dataset as an example, the validation RMSE and MAE of the ten trained models are illustrated in Fig. 6 (a) and (b). It shows that the proposed method with detecting abnormal input achieves lower RMSE and MAE with varying input window sizes, outperforming the results without detecting the abnormal input. The RMSEs of the proposed method with different window sizes, even as short as 10 s, are within 2 %, demonstrating its reliable estimating performance in terms of flexible input lengths. In comparison, the RMSEs of the estimation of the model without detecting abnormal input are larger than 3.4 % for all input window sizes and even over 4.7 % for the window sizes of 10 s and 20 s. Similarly, the MAE of the model with anomaly detection exhibits significant reductions compared to the model without anomaly detection, further highlighting the effectiveness of the proposed approach. Additionally, the results reveal that reducing the window size adversely impacts the traditional method without anomaly detection, while the proposed method remains robust across varying window sizes. Furthermore, the average estimation time per input sample is computed for the model with and without anomaly detection to assess the efficiency of the developed model. The results are presented in Fig. 6(c). It shows that the computing time for both model structures increases as the window size grows. Overall, the traditional SOC estimation model without anomaly detection has a lower computing time than the proposed method because of its simpler structure and fewer model parameters. The maximum computing time of the traditional model of a 100 s window size is 0.12 ms/sample while that of the proposed method is 0.21 ms/sample. Nonetheless, the computing time of the proposed method is small enough to meet an online SOC estimation. The computing time of a 10 s window size for the proposed method is as short as 0.1 ms/sample, which still achieves an RMSE lower than 2 %.

The proposed model uses an encoder to extract latent features from the input data and the extracted features are fed into two branches of SOC estimation and anomaly detection. In comparison, the traditional end-to-end SOC estimation structure extracts latent features only for SOC estimation. The results of the encoder module, both with and without connection to the anomaly detection module, are further examined to understand the interaction between feature extraction and anomaly detection. The encoder module without anomaly detection here is a traditional DNN for SOC estimation.

The t-distributed stochastic neighbour embedding (TSNE) method is used to encode the output features of the encoder module into two components to visualise the features in a two-dimensional space. Taking the 0 °C test dataset as an example, the extracted features of the DNN with and without anomaly detection are shown in Fig. 7 . As shown in Fig. 7(a), in the absence of anomaly detection during model training, the features extracted from the normal and abnormal inputs are not significantly different at the same SOC. This is because the extracted features from both normal and abnormal inputs are mapped solely to SOC, making it difficult to differentiate their feature representations. Consequently, the model is unable to effectively identify the abnormal input from a mixture of normal and abnormal inputs. The ineffective anomaly detection negatively affects the DNN on SOC estimation accuracy, which is consistent to its poorer performance compared to the proposed method. Fig. 7(b) illustrates the feature extraction result in the case of the anomaly detection module is involved in model training. Besides estimating SOC, the trained model primarily reconstructs normal voltages, resulting in distinct feature representations from abnormal ones. As a result, the figure shows a clear distinction between the features extracted from normal and abnormal inputs at the same SOC level. For normal inputs, the features are distributed from right to left as SOC increases, whereas for abnormal inputs, they follow the opposite trend. Since these features are further decoded into different voltage values, the model trained on normal inputs produces significant reconstruction errors for abnormal inputs, which can be detected using a predefined threshold. Therefore, this distinct feature distribution enables the model to effectively identify abnormal inputs, enhancing the overall reliability of SOC estimation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Model generalisation on a new battery type
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the generalisation of the proposed method, this section further validates it on a public dataset collected from a different type of battery [64]. The developed model is retrained and tested on this dataset to evaluate its effectiveness. The dataset comprises tests conducted on a series of LiNiMnCoO2 batteries with a nominal capacity of 3 Ah under various dynamic drive cycles and temperature conditions. The batteries were charged using a constant current (1C) until reaching 4.2 V, followed by a constant voltage phase that continued until the current dropped to 50 mA. Discharge tests were performed using different dynamic drive cycle profiles, including the UDDS, HWFET, LA92, and US06 drive cycles, as well as eight mixed drive cycles, until 95 % of the 1C discharge capacity was depleted. For validation, data from batteries tested under mixed drive cycles at 0 °C, 10 °C, and 25 °C were used, aligning with the conditions in the experimental dataset. The current and voltage profiles from the public dataset under these conditions are shown in Fig. 8 . These profiles demonstrate dynamic and irregular patterns, representing the random load conditions experienced by EVs during real-world operations.

The developed model is trained on data collected from mixed drive cycles at 25 °C and tested using six distinct datasets representing various abnormal conditions. These conditions include 0 °C and 10 °C temperatures, noise, bias, drift, and freezing, as introduced in Sections 4.1 and 4.2. The absolute SOC estimation errors for these six datasets are illustrated in Fig. 9 , with the corresponding RMSEs and MAEs summarised in Table 5 . The results indicate that the proposed method, incorporating anomaly detection, achieves significantly more accurate SOC estimations under various anomaly conditions compared to the method without anomaly detection. Specifically, the proposed method maintains absolute SOC estimation errors within 5 % in nearly all cases. In contrast, the traditional method without anomaly detection exhibits maximum absolute errors exceeding 15 % across all anomaly modes, with errors reaching as high as 100 % for the 0 °C anomaly dataset. Furthermore, the RMSEs and MAEs of the proposed method remain below 2 % and 1 %, respectively, across all types of anomalies, achieving reductions of over 46 % and 42 % compared to the method without anomaly detection. These results underscore the effectiveness of the proposed method in identifying and handling anomalous input data, thereby significantly enhancing the accuracy and reliability of SOC estimation under dynamic and challenging operational conditions.

This work introduces a multi-task DNN capable of simultaneously estimating SOC and detecting abnormal inputs, achieving accurate and reliable SOC predictions under complex operating conditions. By incorporating a self-supervised anomaly detection mechanism, the model is trained exclusively on normal data but effectively generalises to handle diverse abnormal data, including temperature variations, noise, and other input irregularities. This makes the model well-suited for batteries operating in challenging environments. Furthermore, the framework demonstrates flexibility across different RNN architectures. For instance, replacing the LSTM with GRU, BLSTM, or BGRU results in estimation RMSEs of 1.79 %, 1.61 %, and 1.41 %, respectively, when anomaly detection is included, compared to 3.47 %, 3.75 %, and 3.25 % without anomaly detection, as shown in Table 6 . This highlights the flexibility of the proposed method when applied to various RNNs. Moreover, the multitask learning approach offers potential for application to other methods, such as Gaussian process regression or CNNs, provided they can accurately reconstruct input voltage and estimate SOC. Similarly, beyond SOC estimation, the strategy could be extended to other battery state predictions, such as state of health (SOH) and state of power (SOP), further enhancing its value for trustworthy battery state estimation.

Current DNNs for SOC estimation assume that the test data share a similar distribution to the training dataset. Thus, the estimation performance is compromised under unexpected conditions, resulting in unreliable SOC estimation. This study proposes a multi-task DNN that integrates self-supervised anomaly detection alongside SOC estimation. The anomaly detection module monitors the voltage reconstruction error during each SOC estimation, enabling the identification and filtering of unreliable estimation results. In this way, the method achieves trustworthy SOC estimation under complex operational conditions.

To validate the method, experiments were conducted across two temperatures and four sensor fault conditions. The results reveal that the proposed method achieves significantly improved accuracy. The RMSE of the proposed method is within 1.74 % for six test datasets containing anomaly data, whereas the traditional scheme without anomaly detection results in an RMSE as large as 4.38 %, confirming the superiority of the proposed approach. Additionally, the influence of the input data length and the weight between SOC estimation and anomaly detection were investigated. The proposed method maintains an RMSE within 2 % even when the input data length is reduced to 10 s, demonstrating its robustness and adaptability to different operational scenarios. Further validation on a public dataset of LiNiMnCoO2 batteries confirmed the method's effectiveness in maintaining SOC estimation errors below 5 % across various anomaly conditions, with RMSE reductions exceeding 46 % compared to the traditional approach.

Furthermore, to understand the performance improvement, the encoder's feature extraction capability is visualised with and without the anomaly detection module. This analysis supports the rationale for the enhanced reliability of the proposed method. The multi-task learning paradigm introduced in this study demonstrates flexible adaptability to other domains requiring trustworthy predictions, offering promising applications beyond SOC estimation.

The proposed model primarily detects and eliminates the abnormal input according to the reconstruction error. In the future, we will further diagnose the anomaly level of the input data and quantify the reliability of the SOC estimations under unexpected conditions.
